does: This paper investigates how reward models used in reinforcement learning often suffer from oversensitivity, assigning meaningfully different scores to responses that are actually equally good. The authors introduce a theoretical framework showing that even seemingly accurate reward models can exhibit excessive sensitivity to minor differences. They propose a training-free solution using Monte Carlo dropout to discretize continuous reward scores into discrete clusters, reducing oversensitivity without retraining. The paper reframes reward model evaluation around two key dimensions: discriminative ability (distinguishing genuinely different quality responses) and specificity (avoiding false distinctions between equally good responses). Empirically, they demonstrate that this oversensitivity problem leads to poor reinforcement learning policies and that their discretization approach mitigates these failures.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature examining reward models as core components of modern RLHF systems, extending prior concerns about reward model reliability and misalignment. It connects to foundational work on reward modeling accuracy and the challenge of learning from human preferences in the absence of dense feedback signals. The paper responds to earlier observations that reward models can be brittle or poorly calibrated, while also extending research into how reward specification affects downstream policy quality. It relates to work on uncertainty quantification in neural networks (leveraging dropout-based approaches) and connects to broader questions about the continuous versus discrete nature of evaluation signals in learning systems. The discretization approach draws on classical techniques from quantization and clustering while applying them to a modern setting.
enables: This work opens several directions for improving reinforcement learning with learned reward models. The discrete clustering approach could reduce problematic reward hacking where policies exploit fine-grained reward differences that don't correspond to true quality distinctions. The framework for evaluating discriminative ability versus specificity could guide better reward model design and selection in practice. Future work might develop end-to-end training methods that explicitly encourage appropriate discretization during reward model learning. The insights about oversensitivity suggest new research into how reward model properties affect policy stability and generalization. This could also inform work on preference learning, RLHF alignment, and the theoretical foundations of learning from implicit human judgments where ground truth quality is inherently categorical or coarse-grained.
